'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292
== External Links == * [https://www.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp Official Website of Nachikatsuura Town] * [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha Wikimedia Commons: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha] {{Shinto Sidebar}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Nachikatsuura]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha-qq]] [[en:Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha-qq]] {{citation needed|bottom}}[[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Japan]]

The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.

'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292

[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}

'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 9784882696292

== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.

'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. '''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292
== External Links == * [https://www.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp Official Website of Nachikatsuura Town] * [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha Wikimedia Commons: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha] {{Shinto Sidebar}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Nachikatsuura]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:tier 1]]

'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}

'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}'' in qq09<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (qqth century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}

== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (qqth century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}

ISBN 9784882696292

'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}'' in qq09<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (qqth century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. '''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}'' in qq09<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (qqth century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}

revision.text:
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292
== External Links == * [https://www.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp Official Website of Nachikatsuura Town] * [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha Wikimedia Commons: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha] {{Shinto Sidebar}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Nachikatsuura]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]]<!--Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha--> [[en:Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha]] <!--Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha--> {{citation needed|bottom}}[[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Japan]]

Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}

revision.text:
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292

'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}

revision.text:
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉}}
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292

The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.

== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
