'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町|12=draft}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺|12=draft}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|12=draft}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=draft}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=draft}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語|12=draft}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛|12=draft}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=draft}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅|12=draft}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉|12=draft}}
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292
== External Links == * [https://www.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp Official Website of Nachikatsuura Town] * [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha Wikimedia Commons: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha] {{Shinto Sidebar}}

{{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Nachikatsuura]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji|-てんふれえと]] [[Category:s11]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha]] [[en:Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha]] [[ja:熊野三所大神社]] [[zh:熊野三所大神社]] [[sn:umano Sansho Ōmiwasha]]

'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町|12=draft}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺|12=draft}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|12=draft}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=draft}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=draft}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語|12=draft}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛|12=draft}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=draft}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅|12=draft}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉|12=draft}}
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292
== External Links == * [https://www.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp Official Website of Nachikatsuura Town] * [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha Wikimedia Commons: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha] {{Shinto Sidebar}}

'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|ja|那智勝浦町|12=draft}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusanji|ja|補陀洛山寺|12=draft}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|-->{{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|ja|熊野那智大社|12=draft}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=draft}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=draft}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|ja|平家物語|12=draft}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|ja|平維盛|12=draft}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=draft}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|ja|那智駅|12=draft}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|ja|紀勢本線|12=draft}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|ja|南紀勝浦温泉|12=draft}}
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292
== External Links == * [https://www.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp Official Website of Nachikatsuura Town] * [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha Wikimedia Commons: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha] {{Shinto Sidebar}}

<!--Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha--> {{citation needed|bottom}} <!-- | style="background:#ccffcc" |[[九十九王子 (日高町)#小中王子|小中王子]] | style="background:#ccffcc" |[[九十九王子 (日高町)#比井王子|比井王子]] | style="background:#ccffcc" |[[九十九王子 (日高町)#松原王子|松原王子]] --> <!--Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha-->
<!--Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha-->

revision.text:
{{神社 | 名称=熊野三所大神社 | 所在地=和歌山県東牟婁郡那智勝浦町大字浜の宮350 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30_scale:20000|name=熊野三所大神社|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=夫須美大神、家津美御子大神、速玉大神 | 創建=[[欽明天皇|欽明帝]]代 | 社格=村社 | 例祭=[[2月13日]]・[[2月14日|14日]] | 画像=[[画像:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]]
熊野三所大神社本殿正面 }} [[画像:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|鳥居]] [[画像:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|浜の宮王子社跡と夫婦楠]] [[画像:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|振分石]] '''熊野三所大神社'''（くまのさんしょおおみわしゃ）、または'''大神社'''（おおみわやしろ）は[[和歌山県]][[東牟婁郡]][[那智勝浦町]]にある[[神社]]。夫須美大神・家津美御子大神・速玉大神の三神を主祭神とすることが名称の由来とされる。主祭神像三躯は[[重要文化財]]に指定されている（美術工芸品、[[1982年]]〈[[昭和]]57年〉6月5日指定）昭和57年文部省告示第98号。 [[九十九王子]]のひとつである'''浜の宮王子'''の社跡{{efn|王子社殿は[[宝永]]の[[津波]]で流失した}}に建つため、浜の宮大神社（はまのみやおおみわしろ）とも呼ばれる。浜の宮王子の守護寺である[[補陀洛山寺]]が隣接しており、神仏習合の名残をみることができる。境内は浜の宮王子社跡として、国の史跡「熊野参詣道」の一部（[[2000年]]〈[[平成]]12年〉11月2日指定）{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=町指定文化財|format=PDF|publisher=那智勝浦町|accessdate=2010-03-14}}。 == 歴史 == 古くから[[熊野那智大社]]の[[摂末社|末社]]で、『[[中右記]]』[[天仁]]2年（[[1109年]]）10月27日条に「浜宮王子」と見える「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編[1985: 233]。[[平安時代|平安]]後期頃の神像3体（大山祇命、天照大神、彦火火出見命）が伝来していることから[[熊野権現|熊野三所権現]]が祀られていたと考えられているが、[[江戸時代]]中期に著された地誌『熊野巡覧記』は異なる神名を挙げており、時代によって祭神が変化していたようである。江戸時代の沿革は未詳だが、濱ノ宮村の産土神として崇敬されていた。[[明治]]の神仏分離に際して補陀洛山寺から独立して[[村社]]（[[明治]]6年〈[[1873年]]〉）となり、明治末年に現社号にあらためられた。 『[[平家物語]]』巻10に[[平維盛]]が一艘の舟に命運を託して出向するさまが描かれたり、那智大社ほか各所所蔵の[[那智参詣曼荼羅]]那智参詣曼荼羅の遺品は、田辺市・[[闘鶏神社]]本、新宮市正覚寺本など数十点が現存する。（参照:奈良国立博物館編『西国三十三所観音霊場の祈りと美』（特別展図録）、2008、p285）に社前の浜から渡海船で出航する渡海僧が描かれているように、補陀洛山寺とともに[[補陀洛渡海]]の伝承地である。 また、境内には'''振分石'''（ふりわけいし）と呼ばれる石柱が残されている。[[中辺路]]・[[大辺路]]の分岐点には諸説があるが、那智を分岐点とする説によればこの石柱がそうであるという紀南文化財研究会・熊野歴史研究会[2008: 83、95]。 === 摂社 === * 丹敷戸畔命（地主の神） * 三狐神（食物の神） * 若宮跡（浜の宮王子跡） == 文化財 == * 木造男神坐像二、女神坐像一 3躯 - 本殿の中の間に女神（にょしん）像、東西の間に男神像をそれぞれ安置する。女神像は天照大神、東の間の男神像は彦火々出見命、西の間の男神像は大山祇神と伝える。3躯とも胸前で拱手する同様のポーズに表される。いずれもカヤ材の一木造で像高は約60センチメートル。作風から平安時代後期、11世紀頃の作と推定される「新指定の文化財」『月刊文化財』225号、第一法規、1982。重要文化財（美術工芸品、1982年〈昭和57年〉6月5日指定）。 * 浜ノ宮王子跡 - 国の史跡「熊野参詣道」の一部（[[2000年]]〈[[平成]]12年〉11月2日指定）。 * 石燈篭 1個 - 町指定有形文化財（美術工芸品、[[1985年]]〈昭和60年〉8月1日指定）。 == 交通機関 == * [[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]] [[紀勢本線]] [[那智駅]] 徒歩3分 == 周辺情報 == * [[南紀勝浦温泉]] == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、角川書店（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * 紀南文化財研究会・熊野歴史研究会編、2008、『熊野古道大辺路調査報告書 - 田辺市から新宮市まで』、大辺路再生実行委員会 * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1） * 長谷川 靖高、2007、『熊野王子巡拝ガイドブック』、新風書房 ISBN 9784882696292 == 関連項目 == {{Commons|Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha}} * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[中辺路]] - [[大辺路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[佐野王子 (新宮市)|佐野王子]]） - '''浜の宮王子''' - （[[市野々王子]]） * [[補陀洛山寺]] {{九十九王子}} {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くまのさんしよおおみわしや}} [[Category:和歌山県の神社]] [[Category:那智勝浦町の建築物]] [[Category:那智勝浦町の歴史]] [[Category:熊野信仰]] [[Category:九十九王子]] [[Category:和歌山県の重要文化財]] [[Category:和歌山県指定史跡]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]]

Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is [[:en:Fudarakusan-ji|Fudarakusanji]], reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', depicting [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]]’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|qq=}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292
== External Links == * [https://www.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp Official Website of Nachikatsuura Town] * [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha Wikimedia Commons: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha] {{Shinto Sidebar}}

<!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--><!--Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha--><!--Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha--><!-- | style="background:#ccffcc" |[[九十九王子 (日高町)#小中王子|小中王子]] | style="background:#ccffcc" |[[九十九王子 (日高町)#比井王子|比井王子]] | style="background:#ccffcc" |[[九十九王子 (日高町)#松原王子|松原王子]] --><!--Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha--><!--Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha-->

revision.comment: Text replacement - "九十九王子|" to "九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|"
revision.text:
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is [[:en:Fudarakusan-ji|Fudarakusanji]], reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', depicting [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]]’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|qq=}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292
== External Links == * [https://www.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp Official Website of Nachikatsuura Town] * [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha Wikimedia Commons: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha] {{Shinto Sidebar}}

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:和歌山県の重要文化財
revision.text:
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is [[:en:Fudarakusan-ji|Fudarakusanji]], reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', depicting [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]]’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|qq=}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292
== External Links == * [https://www.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp Official Website of Nachikatsuura Town] * [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha Wikimedia Commons: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha] {{Shinto Sidebar}}

revision.text:
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is [[:en:Fudarakusan-ji|Fudarakusanji]], reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', depicting [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]]’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|qq=}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292
== External Links == * [https://www.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp Official Website of Nachikatsuura Town] * [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha Wikimedia Commons: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha] {{Shinto Sidebar}}

== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', depicting [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]]’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|qq=}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]

revision.text:
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
==Overview== {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is [[:en:Fudarakusan-ji|Fudarakusanji]], reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', depicting [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]]’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|qq=}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11568870]] Wikidata name: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
==Overview== {{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is [[:en:Fudarakusan-ji|Fudarakusanji]], reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', depicting [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]]’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudarakutokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]

ISBN 9784882696292

Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', depicting [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]]’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudarakutokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]

'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is [[:en:Fudarakusan-ji|Fudarakusanji]], reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.

The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568870]] Wikidata name: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
==Overview== {{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is [[:en:Fudarakusan-ji|Fudarakusanji]], reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', depicting [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]]’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudarakutokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292
== External Links == * [https://www.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp Official Website of Nachikatsuura Town] * [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha Wikimedia Commons: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha] {{Shinto Sidebar}}

== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', depicting [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]]’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudarakutokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568870]] Wikidata name: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
==Overview== {{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is [[:en:Fudarakusan-ji|Fudarakusanji]], reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', depicting [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]]’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudarakutokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]
