<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568870]] Wikidata name: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
==Overview== {{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is [[:en:Fudarakusan-ji|Fudarakusanji]], reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', depicting [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]]’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudarakutokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292
== External Links == * [https://www.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp Official Website of Nachikatsuura Town] * [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha Wikimedia Commons: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha] {{Shinto Sidebar}}

The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudarakutokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568870]] Wikidata name: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
==Overview== {{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is [[:en:Fudarakusan-ji|Fudarakusanji]], reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', depicting [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]]’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudarakutokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11568870]] Wikidata name: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
==Overview== {{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is [[:en:Fudarakusan-ji|Fudarakusanji]], reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', depicting [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]]’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudarakutokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 9784882696292

The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is [[:en:Fudarakusan-ji|Fudarakusanji]], reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]'', depicting [[:en:Taira no Koremori|Taira no Koremori]]’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudarakutokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from [[:en:Nachi Station|Nachi Station]] on the JR [[:en:Kisei Main Line|Kisei Main Line]].
== Nearby Attractions == * [[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]]

[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusan-ji|en|Fudarakusan-ji|WD=Q2585020|lt=Fudarakusanji}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|WD=Q830740|lt=The Tale of the Heike}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|en|Taira no Koremori|WD=Q3055506|lt=Taira no Koremori}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudarakutokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|en|Nachi Station|WD=Q2584944|lt=Nachi Station}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line}}.

Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
==Overview== {{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusan-ji|en|Fudarakusan-ji|WD=Q2585020|lt=Fudarakusanji}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|WD=Q830740|lt=The Tale of the Heike}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|en|Taira no Koremori|WD=Q3055506|lt=Taira no Koremori}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudarakutokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|en|Nachi Station|WD=Q2584944|lt=Nachi Station}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|en|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|WD=Q11408343|lt=Nanki-Katsuura Onsen}}
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292

'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
==Overview== {{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusan-ji|en|Fudarakusan-ji|WD=Q2585020|lt=Fudarakusanji}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|WD=Q830740|lt=The Tale of the Heike}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|en|Taira no Koremori|WD=Q3055506|lt=Taira no Koremori}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudarakutokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|en|Nachi Station|WD=Q2584944|lt=Nachi Station}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|en|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|WD=Q11408343|lt=Nanki-Katsuura Onsen}}
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292
== External Links == * [https://www.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp Official Website of Nachikatsuura Town] * [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha Wikimedia Commons: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha] {{Shinto Sidebar}}

'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura|1=Nachikatsuura}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusan-ji|en|Fudarakusan-ji|WD=Q2585020|lt=Fudarakusanji|1=Fudarakusan-ji}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|WD=Q830740|lt=The Tale of the Heike|1=The Tale of the Heike}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|en|Taira no Koremori|WD=Q3055506|lt=Taira no Koremori|1=Taira no Koremori}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudaraku tokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.

== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|en|Nachi Station|WD=Q2584944|lt=Nachi Station|1=Nachi Station}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line|1=Kisei Main Line}}.

The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|WD=Q830740|lt=The Tale of the Heike|1=The Tale of the Heike}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|en|Taira no Koremori|WD=Q3055506|lt=Taira no Koremori|1=Taira no Koremori}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudaraku tokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.

'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura|1=Nachikatsuura}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusan-ji|en|Fudarakusan-ji|WD=Q2585020|lt=Fudarakusanji|1=Fudarakusan-ji}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|WD=Q830740|lt=The Tale of the Heike|1=The Tale of the Heike}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|en|Taira no Koremori|WD=Q3055506|lt=Taira no Koremori|1=Taira no Koremori}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudaraku tokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha]][[ja:熊野三所大神社]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
==Overview== {{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura|1=Nachikatsuura}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusan-ji|en|Fudarakusan-ji|WD=Q2585020|lt=Fudarakusanji|1=Fudarakusan-ji}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|WD=Q830740|lt=The Tale of the Heike|1=The Tale of the Heike}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|en|Taira no Koremori|WD=Q3055506|lt=Taira no Koremori|1=Taira no Koremori}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudaraku tokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|en|Nachi Station|WD=Q2584944|lt=Nachi Station|1=Nachi Station}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line|1=Kisei Main Line}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|en|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|WD=Q11408343|lt=Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|1=Nanki-Katsuura Onsen}}

ISBN 9784882696292

'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura|1=Nachikatsuura}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusan-ji|en|Fudarakusan-ji|WD=Q2585020|lt=Fudarakusanji|1=Fudarakusan-ji}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|WD=Q830740|lt=The Tale of the Heike|1=The Tale of the Heike}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|en|Taira no Koremori|WD=Q3055506|lt=Taira no Koremori|1=Taira no Koremori}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudaraku tokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|en|Nachi Station|WD=Q2584944|lt=Nachi Station|1=Nachi Station}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line|1=Kisei Main Line}}.

'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
==Overview== {{Infobox religious building | name = Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | image = [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha02s1920.jpg|280px]] | caption = Main hall of Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha | location = 350 Hamanomiya, Nachikatsuura, Higashimuro District, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|33|38|41.65|N|135|56|3.86|E|region:JP-30|display=inline,title}} | deity = Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, Hayatama no Ōkami | established = Reign of Emperor Kinmei | type = Village shrine (formerly) | festivals = February 13–14 }}
[[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|Torii gate]] [[File:Kumanosanshooomiwasha04s2040.jpg|thumb|220px|Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine and Meoto Kusunoki]] [[File:Kumano Kodo Furiwake-ishi.jpg|thumb|150px|Furiwake Stone]]
'''Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha''' (熊野三所大神社), also known simply as '''Ōmiwasha''' (大神社), is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|qid=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura|1=Nachikatsuura}}, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan. The shrine enshrines the three deities: Fusumi no Ōkami, Ietsumi no Mikoto no Ōkami, and Hayatama no Ōkami, from which its name derives. The three main deity statues are designated Important Cultural Properties of Japan (1982)<ref>Ministry of Education, Notification No. 98 of 1982.</ref>.
It is built on the site of the former '''Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' (浜の宮王子), one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|qid=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is also referred to as '''Hamanomiya Ōmiwasha'''. Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusan-ji|en|Fudarakusan-ji|qid=Q2585020|lt=Fudarakusanji|1=Fudarakusan-ji}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|qid=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|qid=Q830740|lt=The Tale of the Heike|1=The Tale of the Heike}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|en|Taira no Koremori|qid=Q3055506|lt=Taira no Koremori|1=Taira no Koremori}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudaraku tokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|en|Nachi Station|qid=Q2584944|lt=Nachi Station|1=Nachi Station}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|qid=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line|1=Kisei Main Line}}.
== Nearby Attractions == * {{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|en|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|qid=Q11408343|lt=Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|1=Nanki-Katsuura Onsen}}
== References == <references /> == Bibliography == * Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), *Wakayama Prefecture*, Kadokawa Shoten. ISBN 404001300X * Kinan Cultural Property Research Group, Kumano Historical Research Society (2008), *Kumano Kodo Ōhechi Survey Report*, Ōhechi Revitalization Committee * Nishi, Ritsu (1987), *Kumano Kodo Michishirube*, Arao Seibundō * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007), *Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook*, Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292
== External Links == * [https://www.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp Official Website of Nachikatsuura Town] * [https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Kumanosanshooomiwasha Wikimedia Commons: Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha] {{Shinto Sidebar}}

Adjacent to the shrine is {{ill|Fudarakusan-ji|en|Fudarakusan-ji|qid=Q2585020|lt=Fudarakusanji|1=Fudarakusan-ji}}, reflecting remnants of syncretic Shinto-Buddhist practices. The grounds are part of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.town.nachikatsuura.wakayama.jp/div/kyoiku/pdf/bunkazai.pdf|title=Designated Cultural Properties of Nachikatsuura|format=PDF|publisher=Nachikatsuura Town|accessdate=2010-03-14}}</ref>.
== History == Originally a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|qid=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, it is first mentioned as "Hamanomiya Ōji" in the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'' in 1109<ref name="Kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee [1985: 233]</ref>. Three Heian-period deity statues (representing Ōyamatsumi, Amaterasu, and Hikohohodemi) suggest it was once dedicated to the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}. The shrine has undergone several changes in enshrined deities over time.
In the Meiji period, it was separated from Fudarakusanji during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} reforms, becoming a village shrine in 1873 and later renamed Kumano Sansho Ōmiwasha.
== Cultural Significance == The shrine and its surroundings appear in works such as ''{{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|qid=Q830740|lt=The Tale of the Heike|1=The Tale of the Heike}}'', depicting {{ill|Taira no Koremori|en|Taira no Koremori|qid=Q3055506|lt=Taira no Koremori|1=Taira no Koremori}}’s sea departure. The site is also significant for the {{ill|Fudaraku Tokai|ja|補陀洛渡海|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudaraku Tokai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 補陀落渡海|1=Fudaraku tokai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11626847}} voyages depicted in Nachi pilgrimage mandalas<ref>For details on Nachi pilgrimage mandalas, see Nara National Museum's special exhibition catalog, 2008.</ref>.
The '''Furiwake Stone''' (振分石) on the grounds is believed to mark the division between the Nakahechi and Ōhechi routes of the Kumano Kodo<ref>Kinan Cultural Property Research Group & Kumano Historical Research Society [2008: 83, 95]</ref>.
=== Subshrines === * Tanishiki Totobe no Mikoto (Local deity) * Mikogami (Deity of food) * Wakamiya site (Former Hamanomiya Ōji site)
== Cultural Properties == * '''Wooden Statues of Two Male and One Female Deities''' - Identified as Amaterasu (female), Hikohohodemi (male), and Ōyamatsumi (male). All are seated, carved from kaya wood in the ichiboku-zukuri (single-block carving) style, about 60 cm tall, dating to the late Heian period (11th century). Designated Important Cultural Property (1982)<ref>''Newly Designated Cultural Properties'', Monthly Cultural Property Journal No. 225, Daiichi Hōki, 1982.</ref>. * '''Ruins of Hamanomiya Ōji Shrine''' - Part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" (2000)<ref name="Nachi"/>. * '''Stone Lantern''' - Town-designated tangible cultural property (1985)<ref name="Nachi"/>.
== Access == * '''By Train:''' 3-minute walk from {{ill|Nachi Station|en|Nachi Station|qid=Q2584944|lt=Nachi Station|1=Nachi Station}} on the JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|qid=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line|1=Kisei Main Line}}.
